name: Houthis (Yemen)
summary: Day 49. HOUTHIS DAY 48 ZERO CONFIRMED ATTACKS ON COMMERCIAL SHIPPING in Red Sea / Bab el-Mandeb since October 2025 Gaza ceasefire. UKMTO 2026-006 advisory remains in effect but NO new incidents on shipping tracker data. April 7 anonymous 'senior Iranian source' threat that 'if situation gets out of control, Iran's allies will also close Bab el-Mandeb' now 9 days old, UNACTIVATED — appears to have been rhetorical positioning ahead of Islamabad rather than operational preparation. Houthis did participate in April 6 formally coordinated tripartite cruise missile/drone salvo targeting ISRAEL ONLY. Red Sea shipping track remains PRISTINE. Most important non-escalation card Iran holds; preservation on Day 48 continues to signal Iran reserves it for genuine ceasefire-collapse, NOT for ceasefire-period pressure. Houthis specifically warned Bahrain and UAE earlier; no new threats in 48 hours.

signals.content: Abdul Malik al-Houthi declared group 'prepared to enter the war' with 'fingers on the trigger' in recorded address. However, he 'pointedly did not say the Houthis were about to launch attacks' (Maritime Executive). Al-Houthi adopted an 'unusually calm tone' and 'refrained from pledging military action' (Yemen Monitor).

Mohammed al-Bukhaiti (political bureau member, not military commander) told NYT: 'the expansion of the conflict to include other countries, including Yemen, is only a matter of time' and 'our hands are on the trigger.' Ali Al-Emad separately told The Cradle the movement is 'in a state of declared war.' But ZERO attacks followed.

Three weeks into war, no Houthi attacks. 'Mystery of no Houthi attacks on Red Sea ships three weeks into Iran war' (The National). Foreign Policy: 'No missile has been fired, no ship has been struck, and no drone has crossed the Red Sea.' Analysts cite depleted stockpiles, fear of Israeli strikes on Hodeidah, US warnings via Omani mediators, and Iran holding them in reserve as 'insurance policy' (MEI).

Al-Bukhaiti escalated rhetoric: 'all options at the military level are possible,' mentioned naval blockade targeting vessels of 'aggressor countries.' CGTN noted: 'Since the war broke out, the Houthis have so far limited themselves to threats and preparations.' Middle East Forum confirmed physical preparations are real (hospital readiness, launcher repositioning, coastal reinforcement) but 'no confirmed attacks.'

Houthi statement via Saba news agency (their own outlet): 'will not remain on the sidelines' and 'monitoring developments and will take appropriate action.' The Levant Files headlined this as 'Houthis Warn (Again) of Entry into War' — framing it as another warning, not a first-time declaration. Language remains CONDITIONAL, not operational. WSJ reported (via Jerusalem Post) that US and Israel are 'seeking to avoid provoking the Houthis' and Saudi Arabia is actively working diplomatically to keep them out.

VERIFICATION CHECK: As of Day 25, ZERO confirmed Houthi attacks on any target since war began. Every maritime tracker (Skuld, UKMTO), military source (CENTCOM, IDF), and shipping insurer confirms: threats only, no attacks. The 21 confirmed maritime attacks in the conflict zone were ALL by Iranian military forces in Hormuz area, NOT by Houthis in Red Sea (Maritime Gateway). The threat is CREDIBLE (physical preparations real, rhetoric escalating) but UNCONFIRMED as actual military action. Analysts (Soufan Center, Foreign Policy, RUSI): Houthis generating 'value without expenditure' — the threat itself is the weapon.

CONFIRMED ACTION: Houthis launched ballistic missile at southern Israel (Beersheba/Negev) on March 28 — first military action in 28 days of war. IDF confirmed interception by air defenses, no casualties. Houthi military spokesman Yahya Saree claimed responsibility via Al-Masirah TV, targeting 'sensitive Israeli military sites.' CENTCOM struck Houthi positions in response. This was a missile at Israel, NOT a Red Sea shipping attack. Double-chokepoint trigger not yet activated but probability materially increased. (Sources: IDF, Al Jazeera, Times of Israel, CNN, Jerusalem Post, Al-Masirah TV)

Houthis launched SECOND missile and drone attack on Israel within 24 hours of first attack. Spokesman Yahya Saree declared on Al-Masirah TV: 'Yemeni Armed Forces will continue to carry out military operations in coming days.' Both attacks targeted Israel, NOT Red Sea shipping. Double-chokepoint trigger not activated but tempo dramatically compressed vs. Knights' 5-10 day prediction. Air raid sirens also activated across Kuwait, Bahrain, Israel early Sunday. (Sources: US News/AP, Al Jazeera, SBS News, Bloomberg)

Bab al-Mandeb threat now EXPLICIT from multiple sources: (1) Houthi deputy information minister Mohammed Mansour told media 'closing the Bab al-Mandeb strait is among our options.' (2) IRGC-linked Tasnim quoted unnamed military source warning Iran could 'open new fronts at the mouth of the Red Sea' if US conducts ground operations on Iranian islands. (3) Two senior Houthi officials told international media group has 'decided to restart missile and drone operations against maritime traffic.' HOWEVER: as of Day 32, still ZERO confirmed Houthi attacks on Red Sea commercial shipping. Attacks remain Israel-targeting only (2 strikes on March 28-29). Maersk pre-emptively rerouted ME11 and MECL services back to Cape of Good Hope. Double-chokepoint trigger remains UNACTIVATED.

Day 32 — STILL zero confirmed Houthi attacks on Red Sea commercial shipping. Despite: (1) two confirmed attacks on Israel (March 28-29), (2) Bloomberg reports Iran 'actively pressing' Houthis to restart maritime attacks, (3) two senior officials claiming 'decided to restart,' (4) deputy minister naming Bab al-Mandeb closure 'among our options.' Most significant signal: Houthis told Lloyd's List directly they see 'no reason to prevent' Yanbu VLCC trade 'at present' — the 'at present' qualifier preserving shipping card as leverage. Bloomberg: 'divisions within Houthis' leadership' partially explains delay. Maersk, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd, MSC rerouted. Bab al-Mandeb at ~57 transits/24hr. UKMTO threat: CRITICAL.

Day 33: STILL zero confirmed Houthi attacks on Red Sea commercial shipping. Despite: (1) deputy minister naming Bab al-Mandeb closure 'among our options,' (2) two senior officials claiming 'decided to restart' maritime operations, (3) Bloomberg reporting Iran 'actively pressing' restart, (4) IRGC-linked Tasnim warning of 'new fronts at Bab al-Mandeb' — attacks have NOT materialized. Bab al-Mandeb in effective commercial closure due to carrier voluntary withdrawal (Maersk, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd, MSC rerouted), not military blockade. Houthis told Lloyd's List they see 'no reason to prevent' Yanbu VLCC trade 'at present' — preserving shipping card as leverage. Bloomberg: 'divisions within Houthis' leadership' partially explaining delay. Pattern: 28 days of rhetoric preceded first Israel attack; shipping attacks may follow similar delayed trajectory. Day 33 with zero attacks is itself a data point — threat generating commercial cost without expenditure. UKMTO threat level CRITICAL. MARAD Advisory 2026-006 active. EU ASPIDES extended to Feb 2027. (Sources: UKMTO, MARAD, gCaptain, Bloomberg, Lloyd's List)

CONFIRMED ABSENCE: Day 34 of war — ZERO confirmed attacks on merchant shipping in Red Sea or Bab el-Mandeb. Despite announcing war entry (March 28-29) and April 1 statement claiming joint operation with Iran and Hezbollah, no maritime attacks have materialized. Bloomberg (March 30) reported Iran pressing Houthis to resume Red Sea attacks but leadership divisions are real — Washington and Riyadh told European allies they believe Houthis 'currently seek to avoid further escalation.' UKMTO has issued no new attack advisories in the shipping lanes. (Sources: Bloomberg, UKMTO absence of advisories)

RHETORIC: Houthi spokesperson stated 'no reason preventing navigation from continuing.' Deputy information minister Mansour: 'closing Bab al-Mandeb is among our options' — described Houthi strategy as 'conducting this battle in stages.' This is RHETORIC, not operational action — 34 days in, language remains conditional and graduated. Commercial closure of Red Sea/Bab al-Mandeb achieved via carrier voluntary withdrawal, not Houthi military action. (Sources: Yemen Monitor)

Day 35: ZERO confirmed Red Sea shipping attacks. Iran 'pushing Houthis to prepare for renewed campaign against Red Sea shipping, contingent upon further US escalation' per Bloomberg citing European officials. Houthi leadership divisions persist (Bloomberg). EU ASPIDES ready. MARAD 2026-006 active. Trump 'hasn't even started' destroying infrastructure could be the escalation trigger that activates Houthi Red Sea campaign — the conditional link is explicit. (Sources: Bloomberg, MARAD, Al Jazeera, France 24)

Day 37: ZERO confirmed Red Sea shipping attacks. No change since April 3. Bloomberg's report (March 30) that Iran is 'pushing Houthis to prepare for renewed campaign contingent upon further US escalation' remains operative. Trump's F-15 loss and 'all hell' rhetoric constitute the kind of escalation Bloomberg described as the activation condition — but still no attacks. EU ASPIDES on station. UKMTO no new advisories. Houthi leadership divisions persist. CONFIRMED ABSENCE. (Sources: Bloomberg, UKMTO absence of advisories)

Day 38: ZERO confirmed Red Sea shipping attacks. No change. Despite: (1) F-15 shootdown, (2) Trump's 'all hell' rhetoric, (3) deadline extension, (4) 'Power Plant Day' threats — all constituting the 'further US escalation' Bloomberg identified as the activation condition for Houthi Red Sea campaign resumption. EU ASPIDES task force remains on station. The War Zone reported fears that Houthis could effectively shut down Bab el-Mandeb, which would 'choke off Saudi oil exports to the east, exacerbating the oil price spike.' Commercial carriers (Maersk, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd, MSC) remain rerouted around Cape of Good Hope. Houthi leadership divisions persist. CONFIRMED ABSENCE of Red Sea attacks. Israel-targeting missiles remain the only Houthi military action.

Updated assessment: Houthis ARE actively engaging militarily — at least 4 separate missile/drone launches at Israel since March 28 (Beersheba March 28-29, Ben Gurion Airport area claimed April 5 with 'cluster missile'). All intercepted or fell in open areas. Houthi leadership told CNN they are attacking Israel 'independently of Iran' to maintain the separate US ceasefire understanding on Red Sea shipping. However, ZERO confirmed attacks on Red Sea commercial shipping — Day 39 of restraint. UKMTO issued no new shipping attack advisories. The Houthis are calibrating: hitting Israel while preserving the Red Sea shipping card as leverage. This is a more nuanced picture than 'the dog that won't bark' — the dog IS barking, just not at ships. (Sources: Al Jazeera, JPost, UKMTO, INSS)

DAY 39 — STILL ZERO RED SEA SHIPPING ATTACKS, despite participating in tripartite salvo at Israel. Houthis joined Iran + Hezbollah in first formally announced tripartite cruise missile/drone barrage on Israel April 6 — but the Red Sea merchant shipping front remains untouched. EU ASPIDES task force still on station. The 'further US escalation' trigger Bloomberg identified has now been crossed multiple times (F-15 shootdown, 'Power Plant Day' rhetoric, deadline countdown, 15 US wounded at Ali Al Salem) — yet Houthis are firing AT Israel, not at commercial shipping. The dog continues NOT to bark on Red Sea. The bifurcation is intentional and disciplined — Houthi capability and willingness to coordinate is now demonstrated, AND they have proven they can do so without re-opening the Red Sea front. This is the war's most underappreciated escalation/restraint dynamic.

EVENING UPDATE: Day 39 — zero Red Sea commercial shipping attacks continues. Houthi Deputy Info Minister Mansour stated: 'The option of closing the Bab el-Mandeb Strait is a Yemeni option that can be implemented should the aggression escalate savagely' (Soufan Center). Confirms restraint is DELIBERATE and CONDITIONAL — not incapacity. EU ASPIDES on station. MARAD Advisory active. No change in bifurcation: attacking Israel, preserving Red Sea card. (Sources: Soufan Center, Maritime Executive, The National)

DAY 40 — ZERO RED SEA COMMERCIAL SHIPPING ATTACKS, BIFURCATION HOLDS [CONFIRMED ABSENCE]. Day 40 of the war and zero confirmed Houthi commercial shipping attacks in the Red Sea. UKMTO has issued no fresh advisories. Houthis participated in the '99th wave of Operation True Promise IV' on April 7 — combined Iran-Houthi-Hezbollah salvo at Israeli military facilities under codename 'Ya Fatima al-Zahra (PBUH).' The bifurcation thesis (strike Israel directly, leave Red Sea commercial flowing) holds. With the Lebanon exclusion in the new ceasefire, the bifurcation now becomes the test case — will Houthis honour the bilateral US-Iran ceasefire, or use the Lebanon loophole to justify continued strikes on Israel? Iran's 'Supreme Leader' cease-fire order to military presumably extends to IRGC but Houthi command authority is more independent. Watch UKMTO advisories over the next 48 hours for the first verification of compliance.

Day 41 of zero confirmed Houthi Red Sea commercial shipping attacks. No formal Houthi statement endorsing or rejecting the bilateral US-Iran ceasefire — deliberate silence preserving optionality. The National (Abu Dhabi) reports Houthis operating a selective 'tollbooth' system in Red Sea/Bab al-Mandeb, 'allowing only ships that distance themselves from the US or Israel to pass.' The bifurcation thesis (attacking Israel via True Promise salvos, leaving Red Sea commercial shipping flowing) continues intact. (Source: The National Abu Dhabi, absence of attack reports across all outlets)

Abdul Malik al-Houthi issued FIRST formal ceasefire statement, calling it a 'major victory for Iran' and 'Resistance Axis' (Middle East Eye, Press TV). CONDITIONAL: will uphold ceasefire 'as long as the US adheres to commitment to halt aggression.' Will NOT target Saudi Red Sea ports if Saudi Arabia 'adheres to de-escalation.' Senior Houthi official separately stated group would 'attack Israel independently of Iran' but uphold US ceasefire. Day 43 of ZERO confirmed commercial shipping attacks. UKMTO reports 57 vessel transits in 24-hour period in southern Red Sea — normal levels. The bifurcation thesis (attack Israel, leave Red Sea flowing) is now EXPLICITLY ENDORSED by leadership, not just inferred from behavior.

Day 44 of zero confirmed Houthi Red Sea commercial shipping attacks. UKMTO Update 030 (March 1 - April 9): 'No confirmed attacks, suspicious approaches, or reported incidents involving commercial vessels' in southern Red Sea, Bab al-Mandeb, or adjacent waters. ~57 vessel transits in 24 hours — at/near historical normal. Threat level: MODERATE (downgraded from CRITICAL). BELLWETHER: Maersk completed first Red Sea transit in two years — material vote of confidence in bifurcation durability. Maersk had routed via Cape of Good Hope since October 2023; this transit is the strongest commercial validation of the bifurcation thesis to date. Post-ceasefire Israel attacks: last confirmed Houthi strike on Israel was April 6 (UAVs targeting Eilat). No confirmed strikes April 7-11 — possible operational pause during ceasefire window. Al-Masirah framed any pause as chance to 'regroup, not a path to peace.' Three readings: (a) operational pause to see how Islamabad talks develop, (b) Iranian direction to halt during ceasefire window, (c) reporting gap. Needs continued monitoring.

AFTERNOON: Continued silence post-Islamabad talks failure. Zero Houthi statements responding to the failure. Zero UKMTO advisories. Zero confirmed or claimed attacks. Zero maritime security alerts. This silence, in the context of talks failure, is itself a signal -- the Houthis are waiting to see if the ceasefire survives before acting. The bifurcation thesis (attack Israel, leave Red Sea commercial flowing) remains intact at Day 45+. This is the thread most likely to change if ceasefire collapses -- but as of afternoon April 12, it has NOT changed. (Sources: absence of UKMTO advisories, absence of maritime security reports, absence of Houthi media statements)

Day 47 of zero confirmed Houthi missile/drone attacks on Red Sea commercial shipping — streak technically intact. Two developments: (1) UKMTO reported April 12 armed boarding attempt 54nm SW of Hudaydah — 10-12 armed people in skiff, captain deployed flare, attackers fled. NOT attributed to Houthis, appears piracy-style. (2) Houthi Foreign Ministry post-Islamabad statement: called failure 'a new victory for the Islamic Republic and axis of jihad and resistance,' affirmed 'unity of fronts,' declared readiness for 'any upcoming escalation.' RHETORIC, not action. Maersk's Red Sea return now structural (MECL service since Jan 2026). (Sources: UKMTO advisory, Yemen Monitor, Tribune India, Supply Chain Dive)

HOUTHI ATTACKS ON BELLIGERENT-LINKED VESSELS RESUMED [CONFIRMED]. April 7: Houthis claimed attacks on three commercial vessels — British-owned Hope Island, Israeli-owned MSC Grace F and MSC Gina — and two US frigates. April 9: Maersk Yorktown (US-flagged) targeted by anti-ship missile while escorted by USS Mason and USS Laboon in the Gulf of Aden (source: Wikipedia Red Sea crisis, UN Security Council Resolution 2812). The bifurcation thesis is NOT broken — UK is a belligerent (Operation Prosperity Guardian), Maersk Yorktown was US-flagged. NEUTRAL commercial shipping (non-US/UK/Israeli) remains untargeted. Maersk's MECL service (Danish-flagged) continues through Suez. Bab el-Mandeb closure CONDITIONAL: Houthis stated closure if (a) aggression against Iran/Lebanon escalates savagely or (b) Gulf Arab states directly join war. They specifically warned Bahrain and UAE. A senior Iranian source told Reuters April 7: 'If the situation gets out of control, Iran's allies will also close the Bab el-Mandeb.' Stimson Center: the decision 'is ultimately set in Tehran' (source: Time, JPost, Stimson Center, Reuters). Despite blockade meeting stated escalation threshold, Bab el-Mandeb closure NOT enacted — absence signal.

edges.context: Iran's proxy in Yemen. Bloomberg: Iran pressing Houthis to resume maritime attacks but leadership divisions real. Washington and Riyadh told European allies Houthis 'currently seek to avoid further escalation.' April 1 claim of joint operation with Iran and Hezbollah — but zero shipping attacks. Day 34 zero Red Sea attacks.

Day 47: Houthi attacks RESUMED on belligerent-linked vessels (US/UK/Israeli) April 7 and 9. Neutral commercial shipping still untargeted. Bifurcation narrowed but intact — Maersk MECL (Danish-flagged) still through Suez. Bab el-Mandeb closure threatened but not enacted.

Red Sea closure would disrupt Saudi Yanbu bypass exports — the only functioning Gulf export route. Oil could spike beyond $130 if both chokepoints close. Day 34 zero attacks but shipping card preserved as leverage. Washington/Riyadh believe Houthis avoiding further escalation for now.
Two confirmed missile/drone attacks on Israel (March 28-29). Day 33 still Israel-targeting only, not Red Sea shipping. Houthis calibrating — hitting Israel while preserving shipping leverage.

trigger_points.condition: Houthis begin confirmed Red Sea attacks on NEUTRAL commercial shipping (verified by maritime trackers) — Day 47 attacks RESUMED but ONLY on belligerent-linked vessels (US/UK/Israeli). Neutral shipping untargeted. Maersk MECL (Danish-flagged) still through Suez. Bifurcation narrowed but intact. Status: WATCHING — probability modestly increased given resumed attacks on belligerent-linked vessels.
trigger_points.mechanism: Double chokepoint: Hormuz + Red Sea both closed. Cape of Good Hope becomes only route. Shipping rates could double again. Oil spike beyond $130. India faces acute energy crisis. But as of Day 33, this has NOT happened despite 34 days of threats. Leadership divisions (Bloomberg) and delayed-action pattern (28 days before Israel attacks) mean trigger cannot be dismissed.

trigger_points.condition: Houthis launch confirmed attack on Israel (missile/drone reaching Israeli territory)
trigger_points.mechanism: Houthis have now demonstrated capability and willingness to strike Israel directly. First confirmed action after 28 days of rhetoric. Intercepted but demonstrates operational capability from Yemen. Next escalation step would be Red Sea shipping attacks.
